title: Singly Linked List
description: Building a linked list manually with a Node class -- what LinkedList (Collections Framework) does internally, seen from the inside for the first time.
objectives: Understand a Node as an object holding data and a reference to the next Node
Build a singly linked list manually: insert at head, insert at tail, traverse, delete
Compare a manual linked list against ArrayList's array-backed behavior

sections.text: In List: ArrayList vs LinkedList, we used java.util.LinkedList without seeing what's actually inside it. Today we build a linked list completely from scratch, using a Node class, to see the real mechanics that let insertion/removal in the middle be O(1) once you're already there.
An object holding one piece of data and a reference to the next Node in the sequence -- the fundamental building block of a linked list.

sections.title: Treasure hunt analogy
sections.text: Each Node is like a clue card: it holds a value, and points to WHERE the next clue is (next), rather than the clues being physically next to each other. Following the chain means reading one card, then jumping to wherever it points -- this is exactly what current = current.next does.

Unlike an array (recall 1D Arrays), a linked list has NO index-based access -- there's no node[2]. You must WALK the chain from head, one next at a time, to reach any given position. This is the fundamental trade-off previewed in List: ArrayList vs LinkedList.
3. A Proper LinkedList Class: Insert at Head

sections.title: Why insertAtHead is O(1)
points: newNode.next = head links the new node to whatever the list currently starts with
head = newNode makes the new node the new starting point
No shifting of existing elements needed -- unlike ArrayList inserting at position 0, which shifts every element (recall List: ArrayList vs LinkedList)

sections.text: Unlike insertAtHead, insertAtTail requires WALKING the entire list to find the last node (where current.next == null) -- this is O(n), not O(1), for a plain singly linked list with only a head reference. Real implementations often keep an extra tail reference to make this O(1) too, at the cost of some extra bookkeeping.

sections.title: Tracing delete(20) on 10 -> 20 -> 30
steps: head.data (10) != 20, so we don't remove the head itself
current starts at head (node 10); current.next.data is 20 -- MATCH, stop advancing
current.next = current.next.next skips over the node holding 20, linking node 10 directly to node 30

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to update head when deleting the very first node -- deleting anywhere else only requires relinking current.next, but the head itself needs special handling.
Losing the reference to the rest of the list by overwriting next before saving where it pointed -- always capture what you need before reassigning.
Assuming index-based access (list[i]) works on a linked list -- there is no such thing; you must walk from head.
Forgetting insertAtTail is O(n) for a plain singly linked list without a separate tail reference, unlike insertAtHead's O(1).

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a Node in a linked list?
questions.answer: An object holding one piece of data and a reference to the next Node in the sequence -- the basic building block that chains together to form the list.

questions.question: Why is inserting at the head of a singly linked list O(1), while inserting at the tail is O(n)?
questions.answer: Inserting at the head only requires pointing the new node at the current head and updating head to the new node -- constant work. Inserting at the tail requires walking the entire list to find the last node (unless a separate tail reference is maintained), which takes time proportional to the list's length.

questions.question: Why can't you access a linked list by index the way you can an array?
questions.answer: A linked list has no contiguous memory layout with computable offsets like an array does -- each node only knows about the next node, so reaching position i requires walking i steps from head one node at a time.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A singly linked list is built from Node objects, each holding data and a reference to the next node, chained together starting from a head reference. Inserting at the head is O(1) -- just point the new node at the old head and update head. Inserting at the tail is O(n) for a plain list, since you must walk to the end first, unless a separate tail reference is kept. There's no index-based access like an array's arr[i] -- reaching any position requires walking node by node from head. Deleting requires relinking the previous node's next around the removed one, with special handling when the node being removed is the head itself.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: A linked list is a treasure hunt, not a numbered street. Each Node only knows the NEXT clue's location. Insert at head = plant a new first clue, O(1). Insert at tail = walk the WHOLE hunt to the end first, O(n).

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What two things does a Node hold?
Why is insertAtHead O(1) but insertAtTail O(n) for a plain singly linked list?
Why is there no index-based access on a linked list?
What special case must delete() handle when removing the head node?

sections.title: Reverse Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the new head. (Framed here with the ITERATIVE approach, building directly on this lesson's Node class; the recursive approach is covered under the Recursion category.)
constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 5000]
hints: Walk the list once, relinking each node's next pointer to point BACKWARD instead of forward -- you need a prev pointer, since the original next is about to be overwritten.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Store all node values in an ArrayList, then rebuild the list in reverse order.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the values list and new nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: In-place iterative reversal using prev/current pointers, exactly as covered in this lesson's own Node-based examples.

sections.title: Middle of the Linked List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node. If there are two middle nodes, return the second one.

examples.explanation: The middle node is 3, with the rest of the list [4,5] following it.
Two middle nodes (3 and 4) exist -- the SECOND one, 4, is returned.

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [1, 100]
hints: Fast/slow pointers (recall Two Pointers): slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 -- when fast reaches the end, slow is exactly at the middle.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the total length first, then walk halfway through in a second pass.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Fast/slow pointer technique -- a single pass, no length precomputation needed.

sections.title: Linked List Cycle
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it.

examples.input: head = [3,2,0,-4], with a cycle back to node index 1

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 10^4]
hints: Floyd's Cycle Detection (fast/slow pointers): if there's a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually LAP the slow pointer and they'll meet at the same node.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track every visited node in a HashSet -- if a node is seen twice, a cycle exists.
approaches.output: For a linked list with a cycle: true

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Floyd's fast/slow pointer technique -- no extra memory needed; if they ever meet, a cycle exists.
approaches.output: For a linked list with a cycle: true

sections.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
sections.problemStatement: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list by splicing together their nodes. (Framed here with the ITERATIVE approach; the recursive version is covered under the Recursion category.)
constraints: The number of nodes in both lists is in the range [0, 50]
hints: Use a dummy head node to simplify edge cases, and a pointer that always advances to whichever list currently has the smaller value.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all values into a list, sort it, then rebuild a new linked list from the sorted values.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n+m) for the values list and new nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Iterative merge with a dummy head, splicing existing nodes directly -- no new nodes created, no sorting needed.

sections.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a sorted linked list, delete all duplicates such that each element appears only once, and return the sorted list.
constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 300]
hints: Since the list is SORTED, duplicates are always adjacent -- one pass, skipping a node whenever it matches the value right after it.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect distinct values into a Set (preserving order via LinkedHashSet), then rebuild the list.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the Set and new nodes

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: One pass in place -- skip over any node whose value matches the current node's value, relying on the list being sorted.

sections.title: Remove Linked List Elements
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list and an integer val, remove all nodes with Node.val == val, and return the new head.
constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 10^4]
hints: A dummy node BEFORE head simplifies the special case of removing the head itself -- treat every removal the same way, including the first node.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Collect all non-matching values, then rebuild the list from scratch.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: A dummy node before head lets every removal (including the original head) be handled with the exact same relinking logic.

sections.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list, and return its head.
constraints: The number of nodes is sz
hints: Two pointers with a GAP of n between them: advance the first pointer n steps ahead, then move both together -- when the front pointer reaches the end, the back pointer is right before the node to remove.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the total length first, then walk to the node just before the one to remove in a second pass.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Two pointers with a gap of n, moved in a single pass -- no length precomputation needed.

sections.title: Linked List Cycle II
sections.problemStatement: Given the head of a linked list, return the node where the cycle begins, or null if there is no cycle.

examples.input: head = [3,2,0,-4], cycle begins at node with value 2

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 10^4]
hints: First, use Floyd's fast/slow pointers (as in Linked List Cycle) to detect a cycle and find the meeting point.
Then, reset one pointer to head and advance BOTH pointers one step at a time -- they will meet exactly at the cycle's start. This is a mathematical property of Floyd's algorithm.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Track visited nodes in a HashSet -- the first REPEATED node is exactly where the cycle begins.
approaches.output: For a list with a cycle starting at value 2: returns that node

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Floyd's algorithm: detect the cycle with fast/slow pointers, then reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time until they meet -- that meeting point is the cycle's start.
approaches.output: For a list with a cycle starting at value 2: returns that node
